content.text: is built on top of a surprisingly complex low-level scheduling engine — one that determines, with precision, which coroutine runs next, when I/O callbacks fire, and how the CPU time is shared across thousands of concurrent tasks. Understanding this layer gives deep intuition for performance tuning, debugging hangs, and making architectural decisions that go far beyond just knowing the API.
Low-level scheduling in asyncio refers to the internal machinery of the

— specifically, the mechanisms by which the loop decides which callbacks and coroutines to execute at any given moment. This includes the ready queue, I/O polling via system calls, timer management, task stepping, and the interplay between
, and raw callbacks. It is the engine underneath all the high-level abstractions like

behaves unexpectedly — a coroutine that doesn't seem to yield, tasks that never start, performance that degrades under load, or timers that fire late — the cause is almost always in the scheduling layer. Without a mental model of how the event loop actually dispatches work, debugging becomes guesswork.
Understanding the scheduler also unlocks the ability to:
Write custom coroutines that cooperate correctly with the loop
Implement backpressure and flow control in data pipelines
Build production-ready connection pools and rate limiters
Integrate blocking code (threads, subprocesses) without stalling the loop
Tune loop responsiveness for latency-sensitive applications
Think of the event loop as a single-threaded worker running an infinite loop. On each iteration (called a "tick"), it does four things in order:
Run every callback currently in the ready queue (one turn of the "run ready" phase)
Ask the OS if any I/O is ready, with a timeout equal to how soon the next timer fires
Fire any timers whose deadlines have passed

in user code is a potential checkpoint where the scheduler can swap to another task. But the swap only happens if the awaited object is not immediately ready — if a coroutine awaits something already resolved, the loop never yields; the same task continues running until it hits an actually-pending await.
How it works — the event loop tick in detail
on every iteration. This is the heart of the scheduler.
The loop maintains a deque named
— a lightweight wrapper around a callable (callback) and its arguments. On every tick, the loop pops
and calls them one by one.
Critically, the loop takes a snapshot of the queue length at the start of the tick and only processes that many handles — new handles added
this phase are deferred to the next tick. This prevents starvation: a callback that schedules itself indefinitely will not lock out other callbacks forever.
steps through its coroutine exactly one frame per scheduling slot. When a task is created or a future it is awaiting resolves, a
method runs on the next tick.
After draining the ready queue, the loop computes a timeout:
is non-empty: timeout = 0 (poll without blocking, then immediately re-run)
(timer heap) has entries: timeout = seconds until the nearest deadline
Otherwise: timeout = up to a few seconds (the loop can rest if there's nothing to do)
, which is a thin wrapper around whichever OS polling primitive is available —
on Windows. The OS returns a list of file descriptors that are ready for reading or writing. For each, the loop looks up the registered callbacks and schedules them into
This is the only place the event loop can block — and it blocks for at most
seconds, so timers remain approximately punctual.
The loop maintains a min-heap named
with an associated deadline (a float, seconds since epoch, from
). After polling, the loop pops all handles whose deadlines have passed and moves them to

# End of each _run_once()
end_time = self.time() + self._clock_resolution
while self._scheduled:
    handle = self._scheduled[0]
    if handle._when >= end_time:
        break
    handle = heapq.heappop(self._scheduled)
    handle._scheduled = False
    self._ready.append(handle)
mean "sleep for zero seconds and come back immediately in the same tick." It schedules a timer for
, which means the callback fires after the current ready queue is drained and after one poll cycle completes. This is why
is the canonical way to yield control to other tasks.
wraps a coroutine and drives it forward by calling
Returns immediately if the awaitable is already done → the task keeps running without yielding
Registers a callback on the underlying
→ the task suspends; when the future resolves,
The task itself never goes onto
(a regular callback) does. This means the loop sees no distinction between a task and any other callback at the scheduling level.
is the atom of asyncio scheduling. It holds a reference to a callable and its args, and it supports cancellation. When you call

import asyncio

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

# call_soon → goes directly into _ready
loop.call_soon(print, "I run this tick")

# call_later → goes into _scheduled heap
loop.call_later(1.0, print, "I run after ~1 second")

# call_at → same, but absolute time
loop.call_at(loop.time() + 2.0, print, "I run after ~2 seconds")
is a container for a result that does not yet exist. Its internals are simple: a state machine (
), a result/exception slot, and a list of callbacks to invoke when the state transitions to
is called. The loop itself never polls futures — they are purely push-based.
subclass that self-drives a coroutine. Its
The yield protocol between coroutines and the event loop is exactly
to yield the future object up to the Task's

# What `await asyncio.sleep(1.0)` looks like under the hood:

async def sleep(delay, result=None):
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    future = loop.create_future()
    # Schedule the future to be resolved after 'delay' seconds
    loop.call_later(delay, future.set_result, result)
    # Yield the future to the Task; Task will re-run us when the future resolves
    return await future
that returns an iterator can be awaited. The iterator must yield

for a bare yield). This is the extension point that allows custom awaitables — like database drivers, HTTP clients, and synchronization primitives — to integrate directly with the event loop without needing any special loop support.
How coroutine suspension works — the yield chain
When a deeply nested coroutine does
object must bubble up through every
. Python's coroutine protocol handles this automatically via

async def level_3():
    fut = asyncio.get_running_loop().create_future()
    return await fut        # yields fut upward

async def level_2():
    return await level_3()  # transparently forwards the yield

async def level_1():
    return await level_2()  # same

# Task.__step() calls level_1().send(None)
# Python propagates the yield all the way up
# Task sees the Future, registers callback, suspends
This chain is zero-overhead for resolved futures: if
returns without yielding, and the task continues running without ever suspending.
The asyncio scheduler is cooperative, not preemptive. A task that never yields — a tight CPU loop with no
statements — will starve all other tasks and block I/O indefinitely.

# BAD: starves the event loop
async def bad():
    total = 0
    for i in range(10_000_000):
        total += i  # No await — loop never gets control
    return total

# GOOD: yields periodically
async def good():
    total = 0
    for i in range(10_000_000):
        total += i
        if i % 10_000 == 0:
            await asyncio.sleep(0)  # yield control every 10k iterations
    return total

The loop itself does not enforce any per-task time budget. This is in contrast to OS thread schedulers, which use preemptive time slicing. The programmer bears full responsibility for making tasks cooperative.
All three eventually put a callback into
immediately. The callback runs on the same tick if it was added during the ready-queue phase, or on the next tick if added during I/O polling.
call, after at least one poll cycle. Always at least one full tick later.
— the current task suspends for one full tick, letting the poll cycle and any other queued callbacks run first. This is the correct way for a coroutine to yield control.
When to use low-level scheduling APIs
— locks, semaphores, rate limiters where the timing of wakeup must be precisely controlled
, where the transport calls callbacks at the Handle level
— wrapping callback-based libraries (Twisted, libuv bindings) into awaitable futures
to schedule periodic health checks without creating a persistent coroutine
— when the overhead of creating a coroutine frame for each item is measurable and
are built on top of these primitives and should be preferred.
For custom awaitables that need to return control to the loop:
Suppose a legacy library accepts a callback when data arrives. Wrapping it in a

import asyncio

def legacy_read(fd, callback):
    # Hypothetical: calls callback(data) when data is ready
    ...

async def async_read(fd):
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    fut = loop.create_future()

    def _on_data(data):
        if not fut.done():
            loop.call_soon_threadsafe(fut.set_result, data)

    legacy_read(fd, _on_data)
    return await fut
is critical when the callback fires from a non-asyncio thread — it safely appends to
across thread boundaries using an internal pipe/socketpair to wake the selector.
Pattern 3: scheduling work from a thread

import asyncio, concurrent.futures, threading

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()

    def blocking_work():
        import time; time.sleep(1); return 42

    # Correct: run in thread pool, get result as awaitable
    result = await loop.run_in_executor(None, blocking_work)
    print(result)  # 42

asyncio.run(main())
that resolves when the thread finishes. Under the hood,
is used to deliver the result back to the event loop thread.
Avoid creating a long-lived coroutine with
if the overhead of coroutine frames is a concern. A raw

def heartbeat(loop, interval, callback):
    def _tick():
        callback()
        loop.call_later(interval, _tick)  # Re-schedule self
    loop.call_later(interval, _tick)

# Usage:
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
heartbeat(loop, 0.1, lambda: print("tick"))
Real-world example — building a rate-limited task dispatcher
A production LLM inference server needs to dispatch at most 100 requests per second to a backend API, while queuing excess work. This is implemented directly with low-level scheduling:

import asyncio
import time
from collections import deque

class RateLimitedDispatcher:
    def __init__(self, rate: int):
        self.rate = rate             # tokens per second
        self._tokens = rate          # current available tokens
        self._queue: deque[asyncio.Future] = deque()
        self._last_refill = time.monotonic()
        self._refill_handle = None

    def _refill(self):
        now = time.monotonic()
        elapsed = now - self._last_refill
        self._last_refill = now
        self._tokens = min(self.rate, self._tokens + elapsed * self.rate)
        self._drain()
        loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
        self._refill_handle = loop.call_later(1 / self.rate, self._refill)

    def _drain(self):
        while self._queue and self._tokens >= 1:
            fut = self._queue.popleft()
            if not fut.done():
                self._tokens -= 1
                fut.set_result(None)  # Wakes up the waiting coroutine

    async def acquire(self):
        loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
        if self._tokens >= 1:
            self._tokens -= 1
            return
        fut = loop.create_future()
        self._queue.append(fut)
        await fut  # Suspends; _drain() will set_result when a token is available

    def start(self, loop):
        self._refill_handle = loop.call_later(1 / self.rate, self._refill)

    def stop(self):
        if self._refill_handle:
            self._refill_handle.cancel()

# Usage:
async def dispatch(dispatcher, payload):
    await dispatcher.acquire()  # Blocks here if rate limit exceeded
    # ... send payload to backend ...

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    dispatcher = RateLimitedDispatcher(rate=100)
    dispatcher.start(loop)
    tasks = [asyncio.create_task(dispatch(dispatcher, i)) for i in range(500)]
    await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    dispatcher.stop()
This design uses no threads, no polling coroutines, and no busy-waiting. The
chain drives the refill at exactly the right interval;
integrates with the event loop's callback queue directly; tasks sleep efficiently without consuming CPU.
Blocking the event loop with CPU work.

Any synchronous computation that runs longer than a few milliseconds will delay all other tasks, including I/O callbacks and timers. The symptom is timer jitter and high latency on unrelated requests.
This blocks the entire OS thread. Always use
creates a new event loop and blocks the calling thread. Inside an async function, always use
RuntimeError: This event loop is already running

when waking the loop from a thread.
from a non-loop thread causes data corruption. Always use
to run other tasks before a synchronous block.
yields once, allowing one pass through the ready queue. But if that pass schedules more callbacks, they run on the
does not guarantee all pending tasks have been given a chance to run; it only guarantees the current task has yielded once.
Creating thousands of tasks without backpressure.

immediately. Creating 100,000 tasks at once saturates the ready queue, increases memory usage from coroutine frames, and causes all tasks to run interleaved in microsecond-sized slices rather than completing in useful batches. Use semaphores or
with a fixed maxsize to throttle task creation.

judiciously, not liberally. Inserting it in every loop iteration adds unnecessary scheduling overhead. Use it only at the granularity where yielding is actually important — every N iterations for a CPU-heavy loop, or at natural chunk boundaries.
for structured concurrency — it provides better cancellation semantics and error propagation, both of which interact directly with the scheduler's task lifecycle.

with a tight deadline and compare the actual wakeup time against the scheduled time. A healthy event loop should wake within 1–5 ms of the requested time. Consistent latency above that indicates the loop is overloaded with ready-queue work.
For I/O-bound libraries, check whether they use
to integrate with the selector. Libraries that use
directly are the most lightweight; those that spawn background tasks per connection add overhead proportional to connection count.
at the OS interface level, which conflicts with asyncio's selector-based I/O registration and produces undefined scheduling behavior.
The asyncio event loop's per-iteration overhead is dominated by three costs: the number of handles in
, the number of file descriptors registered with the selector, and the timer heap management for
On CPython, the overhead of a single

call is roughly 200–400 ns. A loop processing 10,000 callbacks per tick spends 2–4 ms just on dispatch, before any application code runs. This sets a practical ceiling: a single asyncio loop cannot reliably handle more than ~1–5 million callbacks per second on modern hardware.
(a drop-in asyncio-compatible loop implemented on top of libuv) reduces this to ~50–100 ns per callback by bypassing CPython's object model for internal state. It also uses
on recent Linux kernels, further reducing syscall overhead for I/O-heavy workloads.

import uvloop
asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy())
# Everything else stays the same
For applications beyond ~50,000 simultaneous connections, the selector overhead grows logarithmically with fd count for
(O(1) per ready event, but O(log n) management). Scaling past this typically requires multiple event loops across multiple OS threads or processes, with a load balancer or
Timer precision is limited by the OS clock resolution and the loop's polling timeout. On Linux,
has only millisecond granularity. Sub-millisecond timers specified with
will not fire more accurately than ~1–2 ms regardless of the requested delay.
— libuv-backed event loop, drop-in replacement, 2–4× faster for I/O throughput.
— enables slow callback detection (default threshold: 100 ms), unawaited coroutine warnings, and detailed future/task tracebacks. Essential during development.
— attaches an interactive console to a running asyncio application, showing all live tasks, their current
chain, and loop statistics. Invaluable for diagnosing hangs.
— call stack profiler with greenlet/coroutine awareness. Unlike
, it correctly attributes CPU time to individual coroutines rather than aggregating all async work into the event loop method.
— swaps the factory for new loops. Used by uvloop, anyio, and test frameworks.
— compatibility layer that runs on top of asyncio or trio, providing structured concurrency primitives that translate correctly to either backend's scheduling model.
trio enforces checkpoints (explicit yield points) by design — every I/O primitive is a checkpoint, and any coroutine that runs for too long without hitting one raises a
. This makes starvation impossible to hide but requires adapting all third-party async libraries.
Callback ordering is deterministic within a tick but not across ticks.
If two tasks are both waiting on futures that resolve simultaneously (e.g., two
calls), their relative wakeup order depends on which
was registered first. Code must not rely on ordering between independent tasks.
is called, the task receives a
checkpoint. But if the task is currently in the ready queue (between ticks), the cancellation is delivered on the
step. There is always at least one more tick between requesting cancellation and the task actually stopping. Code that relies on a task being done immediately after

handles are not cancelled when no longer needed, they hold references to their callbacks and arguments indefinitely (until they fire). In long-running servers, accumulating stale timer handles from disconnected clients is a memory leak. Always store the returned
, and selector registrations — is not thread-safe. Any interaction with the loop from outside the loop's thread must go through
. Violating this silently corrupts the ready queue or selector state.
callbacks are caught by the loop and passed to
— they do not propagate to the caller and do not cancel other tasks. This differs from
. Unhandled exceptions in raw callbacks must be logged or the loop's exception handler customized, or they will be silently swallowed (or printed to stderr by the default handler but not re-raised).
The asyncio event loop is a single-threaded scheduling engine running
in a tight loop, with three distinct phases per tick: draining the ready queue, polling I/O, and firing expired timers.
is a potential suspension point, but suspension only actually occurs when the awaited
is not yet resolved — resolved futures cause zero-overhead pass-through.
callbacks on futures, and reschedules itself via
— making it no different from any other handle at the scheduler level.
yields for exactly one full tick, allowing other queued callbacks to run; it does not guarantee all pending tasks have completed a full step.
The scheduler is purely cooperative — a CPU-bound task with no
statements starves all other tasks and blocks all I/O indefinitely.

is a drop-in replacement that reduces per-callback overhead by 4–8× and improves timer precision to sub-millisecond by replacing the Python selector loop with libuv's native I/O event loop.
